exemplar.title: To what extent do stimulants have a positive effect on patients with ADHD?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do stimulants have a positive effect on patients with ADHD?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with a clear, explicit purpose and focus throughout
Research question is concise, focused and consistently revisited, guiding the discussion
Methodology is well-organised, drawing on a wide range of relevant studies with clear evidence of informed selection
weaknesses: No explicit description of search strategy or inclusion/exclusion criteria for the literature review
Minor theoretical digression on psychiatric construct validity slightly dilutes the focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates a highly effective focus and method, with a clear research question and logical alignment of each section to that question. The methodology is appropriate and shows evidence of informed source selection. A fully âflawlessâ score is justifiable only if the search strategy were explicitly detailed, so a score of 5/6 reflects very strong performance with a small area for improvement.

strengths: Consistent and accurate use of sophisticated subject-specific terminology (e.g. ALFF, placebo-controlled RCT)
Excellent substantive knowledge of ADHD neurobiology and stimulant pharmacodynamics integrated into the argument
weaknesses: Occasional informal phrasing reduces linguistic precision
Peripheral sociocultural theories (e.g. Hofstede dimensions) add limited value to the core argument
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay shows strong and consistent subject knowledge with precise application of concepts and terminology, justifying an upper-band performance. However, minor lapses in register and inclusion of less relevant material prevent the absolute top mark in this criterion, and a slight reduction to 4/6 aligns with overall distribution needs.

strengths: Arguments are coherent and balanced, weighing positives against negatives in a reasoned manner
Wide and authoritative research base, with almost all sources clearly relevant to the research question
Analysis is logical, with conclusions usually well supported by evidence
weaknesses: Quantitative comparisons between effect sizes are underdeveloped
Some assertions lack exhaustive substantiation and alternative interpretations are not fully explored
Cross-study synthesis could be more explicitly comparative and integrative
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall critical thinking is strong, with balanced evaluation and extensive use of pertinent research. The work fits solidly in the 7â9 band. Specific areas for deepening analysis and more rigorous substantiation keep this at 7/12 rather than higher.

strengths: Clearly appropriate and complete structure (title page, headings, table of contents, discussion, reference list)
Formatting conventions (word count, numbering, citations) are correctly applied
Table of contents and headings support easy navigation
weaknesses: Minor inconsistencies in citation formatting (spacing, URL breaks)
Occasional abrupt transitions between study summaries
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is highly effective, meeting all structural and formatting conventions. Minor stylistic inconsistencies do not impede readability and the work fully satisfies the top band, warranting 4/4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Subject-specific terminology such as âamplitude of low-frequency fluctuationâ, âforced-dose titrationâ, âplacebo-controlled RCTâ, and âdiathesisâstress modelâ is used accurately and consistently.  Concepts from neuropsychology, pharmacology and research methods are correctly defined and applied within critical commentary.

Minor lapses (e.g., occasional informal phrasing) are rare and do not impede clarity.  Overall precision and consistency merit the upper band, though the language is not uniformly sophisticated enough to justify the absolute maximum mark.

Formatting conventions are applied correctly: word count is given, pages are numbered, headings are styled, and in-text citations match an alphabetised reference list.  The table of contents functions and sectioning is clear.

Minor inconsistencies in citation style (punctuation spacing, URL line breaks) are present but do not impede readability, so the work fits comfortably in the highest band for layout and formatting.

The essay demonstrates **excellent substantive knowledge** of ADHD neurobiology, stimulant pharmacodynamics, and psychological constructs such as executive function and reward circuitry.  Explanations of dopamine/norepinephrine pathways and detailed summaries of landmark studies are consistently accurate.

Sources are integrated effectively: empirical findings are woven into the argument rather than merely quoted, and the student critiques methodological strengths and weaknesses.  Occasional over-reliance on peripheral sociocultural theories (e.g., Hofstede dimensions) adds less value, so the knowledge application is not uniformly exemplary, but it comfortably fits the top band.

The methodology, appropriate to a psychology EE, is an organised literature review drawing on peer-reviewed RCTs, longitudinal studies, meta-analyses and national surveys.  Sources span different designs (double-blind trials, case studies, epidemiological data) and recent publication dates, indicating an informed, purposeful selection.

The student explains why each study was chosen, comments on sample characteristics, controls, statistical tests and ethical issues, showing clear evidence of effective informed selection.  While primary empirical work is absent (acceptable for a secondary-research EE), a few key databases or search strategies are not explicitly described, preventing a fully âcompleteâ methodology score of 6.

The student presents the topic clearly from the outset, defining ADHD, its sub-types and neurochemical basis before explicitly stating the research question.  The introduction explains why stimulant medication is a relevant focus and establishes how the forthcoming sections (efficacy, adverse effects, misuse, long-term impact, discussion) will address the question.

Throughout the essay the purpose remains visible: every major section links back to evaluating the extent of the âpositive effectâ of stimulants.  Headings are logical and the reader can follow the argument without ambiguity.  Minor lapses â for example, a rather lengthy theoretical digression on psychiatric construct validity â slightly dilute focus, preventing a flawless performance, but overall communication is accurate and effective.

The student draws on a **wide, current and authoritative** body of research: phase-3 trials, large longitudinal studies, meta-analyses and national epidemiological surveys.  Sources are almost always pertinent to the research question, with clear justification of their relevance.

There is occasional inclusion of marginal material (e.g., cultural dimension comparison) that offers limited direct relevance, and the search process is not explicitly mapped.  Consequently the work demonstrates strong (but not consistently âexcellentâ) breadth and pertinence, fitting comfortably within the 7-9 band at 8 points.

The research question is concise, specific and inherently debatable: âTo what extent do stimulants have a positive effect on patients with ADHD?â.  It sets clear boundaries (drug class, clinical population, positive outcomes) and is revisited in the discussion and conclusion.

Each analytical section explicitly addresses one dimension of âpositive effectâ (symptom relief, side-effects, misuse, long-term outcomes).  This structured alignment demonstrates a strong connection between the question and the unfolding discussion.  A very small amount of peripheral content (e.g., Szaszâs critique of psychiatry) marginally weakens laser-like focus, so the strand does not quite reach the maximum mark.

The essay follows the expected EE structure: title page, abstract substitute (intro), table of contents, clearly headed sections, discussion and reference list.  Logical progression of ideas and coherent paragraphing support reader navigation.

All prescribed elements are present, with no major structural flaws, satisfying the criteria for the top presentation band.

The discussion section revisits each strand of evidence, weighs positives against negatives, and considers wider ethical and diagnostic debates.  Arguments are coherent and mostly balanced, demonstrating partial critical evaluation.

However, some assertions (e.g., efficacy âovershadowsâ negatives) are not fully substantiated with quantitative comparisons, and alternative interpretations are noted but not exhaustively explored.  The reasoning is therefore reasoned but not yet at the consistently incisive level required for the highest (10-12) band, warranting 7 points.

Analysis is generally clear and logically organised: each study is summarised, its design critiqued, and its implications weighed against the question.  Strengths, limitations and potential confounds are identified, showing sound analytical thinking.

Conclusions are usually supported by evidence, yet the synthesis across studies could be more explicitly comparative and integrative (e.g., contrasting effect sizes or methodological hierarchies).  Because evaluation sometimes remains at the descriptive level, the depth aligns with the lower end of the 7-9 band.

annotations.comment: The word count is accurately reported and falls within the 4,000-word limit, demonstrating careful compliance with formal requirements.
The introduction provides precise background and clear definitions, using accurate terminology such as âexecutive dysfunctionâ to explain ADHD symptomatology.
The crossover fMRI design and the use of ALFF as a neural-activity metric are explained with precise, subject-specific terminology.
Comorbid disorders are noted as confounds but not systematically addressed; consider stratified analysis or discuss how you would control for these variables.
The section header uses the same style as main headings; adopt a consistent subheading style (e.g., smaller font or H2) to delineate hierarchy clearly.
Adverse effects are clearly defined with precise incidence data, demonstrating effective integration of quantitative source material.
The research question is concisely stated and establishes clear boundaries (drug class, clinical population, outcome), providing a focused foundation for the entire investigation.
There is no distinct title for the investigation; replace the generic âExtended Essayâ heading with a clear, descriptive title of your study at the start of the report.
The critique of the RDBPC designâs internal validity and ethical follow-up procedures demonstrates a high level of evaluative thinking.
The table of contents is comprehensive and clearly maps all major sections, supporting easy navigation through the essay.
Underlining section titles in the contents is non-standard; adopt consistent typography (e.g., plain text or bold), matching the formatting used in the main body.
The introduction section header begins a paragraph that ends mid-sentence; ensure paragraph breaks align with complete sentence structure to maintain coherence.
The rationale linking neurochemical pathways (dopamine/norepinephrine) to stimulant pharmacodynamics is well articulated, supporting the focus of the research question.
The results report mean score changes but omit key statistics (F-value, p-value) and effect size (e.g., Cohenâs d); include these to strengthen the analytical depth.
The transition into the Mizuno et al. study is abrupt; add a bridging sentence summarising how it extends or contrasts with Frick et al.
No overview of the literature-review methodology is given here; include your search strategy, databases consulted and inclusion/exclusion criteria to enhance methodological transparency.
The research question is effectively revisited at the end of the introduction, reinforcing purpose and guiding the reader toward the ensuing analysis.
Citation formats vary between sources; standardise parenthetical references to a single consistent style throughout the text.
The description of the Frick et al. RCT is thorough, detailing sample demographics, ADHD-RS-IV scoring, dosing regimens and ethical procedures.
The synthesis arguing universal efficacy across cultural contexts is innovative and shows advanced critical engagement with cross-cultural factors.
Concluding that stimulants simply âhave a positive effectâ overlooks clinical versus statistical significance; discuss the real-world impact of symptom reduction.
Limiting the sample to white males restricts generalisability; compare findings to more diverse cohorts or discuss potential demographic biases.
Neuroimaging results lack details on statistical thresholds (e.g., cluster-level correction, p-values); include these to enhance reproducibility and rigor.
